chunk_summary: This section focuses on micronutrient, hydration, and tube feeding strategies in cystic fibrosis (CF), emphasizing proactive supplementation, monitoring schedules, and management of gastrointestinal complications to optimize nutrition and growth in CF patients.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of proactive micronutrient supplementation in CF care, including fat-soluble vitamins and sodium.
Learn appropriate dosing ranges and monitoring schedules for vitamins A, D, E, and K in CF patients.
Recognize strategies for managing gastrointestinal complications such as DIOS, reflux, and vomiting in CF.
Explore the use of overnight NG or gastrostomy feeds to meet growth targets in CF patients.
key_facts: Fat-soluble vitamin dosing: Vitamin A (0–12 months) 750–1500 µg RAE/day; Vitamin D (infants) 400–500 IU/day, older children 800–2000 IU/day (up to 4000 IU in selected cases); Vitamin E (0–12 months) 40–80 IU/day; Vitamin K (0–12 months) 0.3–0.5 mg/day.
Sodium supplementation: Baseline 1–2 mmol/kg/day in infants, increased to up to 4 mmol/kg/day during high-loss states (e.g., hot weather, fever, diarrhea).
Salt depletion can cause dehydration and hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis (pseudo-Bartter phenotype).
Monitor fat-soluble vitamin levels 3–6 months after starting or changing supplements, then annually where feasible.
Overnight NG or gastrostomy feeds can provide 30–50% of caloric needs when growth targets are unmet despite confirmed adherence.
reference_quote: Fat-soluble vitamins should be monitored 3–6 months after starting or changing supplements and then at least annually where feasible. (ESPEN–ESPGHAN–ECFS guideline on nutrition care for cystic fibrosis)
document_key_topics: Hydration and sodium management in CF
Tube feeding strategies for growth optimization in CF
